transcription.subject: social_reciprocity
transcription.raw_text:
Turn-Taking Tally - Name: Jaylen - Session: 1:1 with therapist - Activity: Thomas trains - Date: 2026-04-10
1. Pass Gordon -> Yes -> brief wait, smiled
2. Pass Percy -> No -> grabbed back quickly
3. Share track -> Yes -> allowed adult turn
4. Pass Gordon -> No -> protested briefly
5. Build tunnel -> Yes -> longest wait so far
Total waited: 3 / 5 (60%)
transcription.teacher_notes: 1:1 therapist session with Thomas trains. Jaylen successfully took turns in 3 of 5 opportunities — a real improvement from the start of March, when he was only hitting 1 in 5. The two misses were both with his favorite engine (Gordon) on one pass and Percy on another — losing his favorite engines is still the hardest turn-taking case. The 'longest wait so far' note on the tunnel-building activity is the notable gain: he stayed regulated and engaged during a collaborative step, which is a new skill.
engagement_indicators: brief protest but no full meltdown on 2 missed turns
Gordon and Percy still the hardest items to share
smiled during the Gordon pass success

matched_goals.reasoning: This is the exact measurement instrument specified in Jaylen's G3 goal: therapist observation of turn-taking during preferred activities (trains), 4 out of 5 opportunities per session. Today he hit 3 of 5, which is 60% and below the 80% target — but up from the 40% trial on 2026-03-29.

goal_mapping.overall_summary: Jaylen is at 60% on G3 turn-taking today — below target but trending the right direction, with a notable gain in sustained collaborative play during the tunnel-building step.

analyses.progress_note: Jaylen is at 60% on Goal G3 (turn-taking / social reciprocity) in today's 1:1 therapy session. That is below his 80% target but represents a real pattern shift from the start of March — he was at 20% twice in a row on 03-15 and 03-22, hit 40% on 03-29, then jumped to the 80% therapist-observed level on 04-03. Today's 60% sits between those two data points. The story is specific to Jaylen: his top interest is Thomas the Tank Engine, specifically Gordon and Percy. Both missed turns today were exactly those engines — Percy (grabbed back immediately) and Gordon on the second pass (brief protest). Losing his highest-value items is still the hardest turn-taking case. The real signal, though, is the tunnel-building step marked 'longest wait so far' — Jaylen stayed regulated and engaged through a collaborative construction step with the adult, which is a different behavior than passing an item back and forth. He is starting to tolerate collaborative play, not just forced turn-taking.
analyses.recommendation: Keep the 1:1 therapist condition the same — Thomas trains as the target activity is working because it is motivating enough for Jaylen to stay in the interaction. For the next session, try a non-Gordon, non-Percy engine as the initial turn-taking pair (Thomas himself, or James) to scaffold success before re-introducing his two favorites. Expand the tunnel-building collaborative step by another minute next session — that is where the biggest gain lives.
analyses.thinking:
Jaylen's G3 progression (turn-taking during preferred activities, 5-opportunity sessions):
1. Baseline (2025-10-15): 20%
2. 2026-03-15: 20% — resists peers, monopolizes spinning toy
3. 2026-03-22: 20% — parallel play only
4. 2026-03-29: 40% — 1:1 adult turn-taking with trains improving
5. 2026-04-01: 60% — brief peer exchanges emerging
6. 2026-04-03: 80% — therapist session, TARGET REACHED
7. 2026-04-10: 60% — today

The key detail for Jaylen is item-specificity. His profile is extremely clear: 'Thomas the Tank Engine (specifically Gordon and Percy)' — those two engines are named explicitly as his highest-value items. Today's misses were:
- Item 2 (Percy): grabbed back immediately. This is losing his second-favorite engine.
- Item 4 (Gordon, second pass): protested briefly. This is losing his favorite engine on a re-ask.

The three successes involved:
- Gordon on the FIRST pass (he had not yet had to repeat the behavior)
- A generic track segment (not a named engine)
- Tunnel building (collaborative construction, not item handover)

This tells me Jaylen's actual turn-taking ceiling is 'one pass of a preferred item, then collaborative play.' That is different from the IEP goal wording ('4 out of 5 consecutive opportunities'). The goal wording pushes for repeated handovers of the same item, and the data says repeated handovers of Gordon/Percy specifically is where he breaks.

Also worth noting: his sensory profile lists deep pressure and spinning as seeks, and his calming strategies are body sock, sensory swing, and chew tube. None of those were needed today — he protested briefly on item 4 but did not need a full sensory reset. That is a regulation gain even on a 60% session.

Confidence: Medium. The trend is up overall but the session-to-session variance is still high (80 -> 60), and the ceiling is item-specific rather than skill-general.
